output.rationale: The findings from the researcher node provide the birth date, death date, and specific foundational contributions to information theory.

prompt_sent:
You are the Distiller skill. You receive raw text (typically the
`findings` of one or more Researcher nodes, or the `chunks` of a
Retriever node) and produce a small structured record.

You make no tool calls. You do no web access. Everything you need is
already in the prompt under INPUTS.

Procedure:
  1. Identify what fields the user's question implies (people, dates,
     numbers, comparisons, percentages, attributions).
  2. Pull those fields out of the inputs.
  3. Emit a compact JSON record. Fields with no evidence in the inputs
     are omitted, not made up.

Output schema (JSON, no prose, no markdown fences):

  {
    "fields": { "<field_name>": "<value>", ... },
    "rationale": "<one short sentence saying which input supports each field>"
  }

Notes:
  - The fields dictionary is the load-bearing output; downstream
    Formatter nodes read it.
  - When the question is a comparison (`fastest growing`, `largest`),
    emit a `comparison` key with `winner: <id>` and `reason: <short>`.
  - When the question's evidence is missing, set `fields: {}` and put
    the gap in `rationale`. Do not invent.

A Critic node may run after you. Its evaluation will fail if you
invented fields or made claims unsupported by the inputs.

USER_QUERY: Fetch https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Shannon and tell me his birth
date, death date, and three key contributions to information theory.

INPUTS:
[
  {
    "id": "n:2",
    "kind": "upstream",
    "skill": "researcher",
    "output": {
      "question": "Fetch https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Shannon and tell me his birth date, death date, and three key contributions to information theory.",
      "sources": [
        {
          "url": "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Shannon",
          "title": "Claude Shannon - Wikipedia"
        },
        {
          "url": "https://www.britannica.com/biography/Claude-Shannon",
          "title": "Claude Shannon | Father of Information Theory, American ..."
        }
      ],
      "findings": "Claude Elwood Shannon was born on April 30, 1916, and passed away on February 24, 2001.\n\nShannon is widely recognized as the father of information theory, famously established in his 1948 paper, \"A Mathematical Theory of Communication.\" His foundational contributions include the quantification of information through the concept of \"entropy,\" which measures the uncertainty or information content of a source.\n\nAdditionally, he established the fundamental limits on data compression (source coding theorem) and the maximum rate at which information can be reliably transmitted over a noisy communication channel (the Shannon-Hartley theorem/channel capacity). His early work also laid the essential groundwork for modern digital circuit design by demonstrating the application of Boolean algebra to electrical switching circuits."
    }
  }
]
